faq.question: What is a good corn yield per acre?
faq.answer: The U.S. national average corn yield is roughly 175-180 bushels per acre. Top producers in Iowa and Illinois often exceed 200 bu/acre with optimal conditions, while dryland farms in drier regions may average 120-150 bu/acre.

faq.question: How do soil and weather affect crop yield?
faq.answer: Soil quality (organic matter, drainage, pH) can swing yields 20-30%. Weather during pollination is critical — drought stress at tasseling can cut corn yields by 40% or more. Excessive rain causes root issues and disease pressure.

tagline: Calculate water needs per acre for your crop
keywords: irrigation calculator, water usage per acre, crop water needs, irrigation cost
calcFn: var eff = parseFloat(efficiency); var net = Math.max(0, cropET - rainfall); var gross = net / eff; var acreFeet = (gross / 12) * acres; var gallons = acreFeet * 325851; var cost = acreFeet * waterCost; document.getElementById('netNeed').textContent = fmt(net, 1) + ' in/season'; document.getElementById('grossNeed').textContent = fmt(gross, 1) + ' in/season'; document.getElementById('totalGallons').textContent = fmt(gallons, 0) + ' gal'; document.getElementById('totalCost').textContent = dollar(cost);

faq.question: How much water does corn need per season?
faq.answer: Corn typically requires 20-28 inches of water per growing season. Peak demand during tasseling and silking can reach 0.35 inches per day. Soybeans need 18-22 inches, and wheat needs 15-20 inches.

faq.question: What irrigation method is most efficient?
faq.answer: Drip irrigation is the most efficient at 90-95%, followed by center pivot at 80-90%, sprinkler at 70-80%, and flood irrigation at 50-65%. However, drip systems have higher installation costs and are mainly used for high-value crops.

tagline: Budget your fertilizer costs by nutrient needs

faq.question: How much nitrogen does corn need?
faq.answer: Corn typically requires 1.0-1.2 lbs of nitrogen per bushel of expected yield. For a 200 bu/acre target, that is 200-240 lbs N/acre. Credit nitrogen from previous legume crops (soybean credit is typically 40-50 lbs N/acre).

faq.question: What is the most expensive fertilizer nutrient?
faq.answer: Nitrogen is typically the largest fertilizer expense, representing 50-65% of total fertilizer cost for corn. Prices fluctuate with natural gas costs since most N fertilizer is synthesized using the Haber-Bosch process.

faq.question: How much does it cost to feed a cow per day?
faq.answer: Feed costs for beef cattle range from $2.50 to $5.00 per head per day depending on feed prices and ration. Dairy cows consuming a TMR (total mixed ration) may cost $6-10/day. Feed typically represents 60-70% of total production cost.

faq.question: How many pounds of feed does a beef cow eat daily?
faq.answer: Cattle eat roughly 2-3% of their body weight daily in dry matter. A 1,200 lb cow eats 24-36 lbs/day. Finishing cattle on high-energy rations may eat 25-30 lbs, while cows on hay may eat 26-30 lbs of as-fed hay.

faq.question: What is the typical useful life of a tractor?
faq.answer: Farm tractors typically have a useful life of 10,000-15,000 hours or 12-20 years. A tractor used 500 hours/year lasts about 20 years. Combines are typically 10-15 years due to higher wear. Salvage value is usually 10-20% of purchase price.

faq.question: Can I use Section 179 for farm equipment?
faq.answer: Yes. Section 179 allows farmers to deduct the full purchase price of qualifying equipment in the year of purchase rather than depreciating over time. The 2024 limit is $1,220,000. This can significantly reduce tax liability in high-income years.

Drying Cost = Bushels x Cost/Point x Moisture Points Removed

faq.question: Is on-farm grain storage worth the investment?
faq.answer: On-farm storage typically costs $1.50-2.50/bushel in bin construction but saves $0.03-0.06/bu/month vs. commercial rates. With a 50,000 bu bin, breakeven is often 7-10 years. The marketing flexibility to sell when prices peak often justifies the investment.

faq.question: How much does commercial grain storage cost?
faq.answer: Commercial elevator storage runs $0.03-0.06 per bushel per month, plus receiving/handling charges of $0.10-0.25/bu. Drying fees are $0.03-0.06 per bushel per point of moisture removed. Total costs for 6 months of storage with drying can reach $0.50-0.75/bu.

faq.question: How is cryptocurrency taxed?
faq.answer: The IRS treats crypto as property. Selling, trading, or spending crypto triggers a taxable event. Short-term gains (held < 1 year) are taxed as ordinary income (10-37%). Long-term gains (held > 1 year) get preferential rates of 0%, 15%, or 20%.

faq.question: Do I owe tax on crypto-to-crypto trades?
faq.answer: Yes. Swapping one cryptocurrency for another (e.g., BTC to ETH) is a taxable event. You must calculate gain/loss based on the fair market value at the time of trade. This applies even if you never converted to USD.

faq.question: How much electricity does a Bitcoin miner use?
faq.answer: Modern ASIC miners like the Antminer S21 draw 3,000-3,500 watts and produce 100-200 TH/s. Running 24/7, that is 72-84 kWh per day or about 2,200-2,500 kWh per month. At $0.10/kWh, monthly electricity runs $220-250 per unit.

faq.question: What electricity rate makes mining profitable?
faq.answer: Profitability depends heavily on BTC price and network difficulty. Generally, rates below $0.07/kWh are competitive for Bitcoin mining. Industrial miners often locate near cheap hydroelectric or stranded natural gas at $0.03-0.05/kWh.

faq.question: What is a good staking APY?
faq.answer: Staking APYs vary widely. Ethereum staking yields about 3-5% APY. Other networks offer higher rates: Cosmos 15-20%, Polkadot 10-14%, Solana 6-8%. Very high APYs (50%+) often come with high inflation that dilutes token value.

faq.question: Are staking rewards taxable?
faq.answer: Yes. The IRS considers staking rewards as ordinary income, taxable when received at fair market value. When you later sell staked tokens, you also owe capital gains tax on any price appreciation from the income recognition date.

faq.question: How often should I rebalance my crypto portfolio?
faq.answer: Most strategies rebalance monthly, quarterly, or when allocations drift more than 5% from targets. Frequent rebalancing incurs more fees and tax events. Threshold-based rebalancing (only when drift exceeds 5-10%) is often more efficient.

faq.question: Does rebalancing improve returns?
faq.answer: Rebalancing primarily manages risk by preventing overexposure to volatile assets. It forces a buy-low-sell-high discipline. Studies show rebalancing can improve risk-adjusted returns by 0.5-1.5% annually in diversified portfolios.

faq.question: What is a good gas price for Ethereum?
faq.answer: Gas prices fluctuate based on network demand. Under 20 gwei is considered low, 20-50 gwei is moderate, and above 100 gwei is high. Weekends and early morning UTC tend to have lower gas prices. Use gas trackers to find optimal timing.

faq.question: Why do smart contract interactions cost more gas?
faq.answer: Simple ETH transfers use 21,000 gas. ERC-20 token transfers use ~65,000. DEX swaps use 100,000-300,000. NFT mints can use 100,000-250,000. Complex DeFi interactions may use 500,000+ gas due to the computational steps involved.

faq.question: What defines a crypto whale?
faq.answer: There is no strict definition, but generally: holding/moving over $10M in a single cryptocurrency is considered a whale. For Bitcoin, owning 1,000+ BTC qualifies. About 2,000 addresses hold more than 1,000 BTC, controlling roughly 40% of all Bitcoin.

faq.question: Do whale transactions affect price?
faq.answer: Large on-chain transfers do not directly move prices, but whale movements to exchanges often signal selling intent and can trigger panic. A whale depositing $50M+ to an exchange typically correlates with 2-5% price drops within 24 hours.

faq.question: What is a normal ink cost per page?
faq.answer: For inkjet printers, typical costs are $0.05-0.10 per black page and $0.15-0.25 per color page at 5% coverage. Laser printers are cheaper at $0.02-0.04 for black. Ink tank printers (EcoTank, MegaTank) can drop to $0.01 per page.

faq.question: What does 5% coverage mean?
faq.answer: 5% coverage means about 5% of the page area is covered with ink, equivalent to a typical business letter. Photos are 20-35% coverage. Marketing materials run 15-25%. Higher coverage uses ink proportionally faster, cutting cartridge yield.

faq.question: What is standard bleed for printing?
faq.answer: The industry standard bleed is 0.125 inches (1/8 inch or 3mm) on all sides. Some large format printing requires 0.25-0.5 inch bleed. Always check with your printer, as requirements vary by equipment and finishing method.

faq.question: What is a safe margin?
faq.answer: The safe margin (or safe zone) is the area inside the trim where critical content like text and logos should stay. Standard safe margin is 0.25 inches (1/4 inch) from the trim edge. This accounts for slight cutting variations during finishing.

faq.question: What is a signature in printing?
faq.answer: A signature is a large sheet printed on both sides, then folded and trimmed to create a section of consecutive pages. A 16-page signature prints 8 pages per side on one sheet. Multiple signatures are gathered and bound together to form the book.

faq.question: Why must booklet page counts be multiples of 4?
faq.answer: Because each sheet of paper creates 4 pages (front and back, two sides of the fold). Saddle-stitched booklets must have page counts divisible by 4. If your content does not fill evenly, blank pages are added at the end or distributed throughout.

faq.question: What is the typical cost per square foot for large format printing?
faq.answer: Standard vinyl banners run $5-10/sqft. Photo-quality posters on satin paper are $6-12/sqft. Canvas prints run $8-15/sqft. Rigid substrates (foam board, ACM) add $3-8/sqft. Volume discounts of 15-30% are common on orders of 10+.

faq.question: What resolution do I need for large format prints?
faq.answer: For viewing at 3+ feet, 150 DPI is sufficient. Posters viewed at arm's length need 200-300 DPI. Billboards viewed from 50+ feet need only 25-50 DPI. Always work at final size — do not scale up a small image.

faq.question: How much ink does screen printing use per shirt?
faq.answer: A typical single-color print (12x14 at 30% coverage) uses about 0.5-1.0 oz of ink. Multi-color designs with higher coverage can use 2-4 oz per shirt. White underbase on dark garments doubles ink usage for those areas.

faq.question: What is a good screen mesh count for t-shirts?
faq.answer: 110 mesh is standard for spot color on dark shirts. 156 mesh works well for detailed spot color on light shirts. 200-230 mesh is used for process color (CMYK) and fine detail. Higher mesh = less ink deposit = finer detail.

faq.question: Is DTG cheaper than screen printing?
faq.answer: DTG is cheaper for small runs (under 25-50 shirts) because there are no screen setup costs. Screen printing becomes cheaper at higher volumes due to lower per-unit ink costs. The crossover point is typically 25-75 shirts depending on colors.

faq.question: What is the average DTG cost per shirt?
faq.answer: Total DTG cost ranges from $5-12 per shirt including ink ($1-3), pretreatment ($0.25-0.75), garment ($3-6), and labor ($0.50-2). Dark garments cost 50-100% more due to white underbase ink requirements.

faq.question: What is a board foot?
faq.answer: A board foot is a unit of lumber volume equal to 144 cubic inches — a piece 1 inch thick, 12 inches wide, and 12 inches long. Hardwood is sold by the board foot, while softwood is often sold by the linear foot. Rough lumber is measured before planing.

faq.question: How much waste should I plan for?
faq.answer: Plan 10-15% waste for experienced woodworkers with clear lumber. Add 20-25% for beginners or lumber with defects. Projects requiring many small parts from large boards can waste 25-30%. Always buy extra rather than run short mid-project.

faq.question: How much does wood shrink?
faq.answer: Wood shrinks most tangentially (across growth rings, 6-12% from green to oven-dry), less radially (3-6%), and almost none longitudinally (<0.1%). A flat-sawn 8-inch white oak board can move 1/8 inch or more between summer humidity and winter dryness.

faq.question: What moisture content should wood be for furniture?
faq.answer: Indoor furniture should be built with wood at 6-8% MC. Workshop lumber should acclimate to the environment where it will live. Wood at 12% MC used indoors (where equilibrium is 6-8%) will shrink noticeably and may crack or warp.

faq.question: What angle do I set my miter saw for a picture frame?
faq.answer: A picture frame has 4 sides with 90-degree corners. The miter angle is 45 degrees. Set your miter saw to 45 degrees. For pentagons set to 36 degrees, hexagons to 30 degrees, and octagons to 22.5 degrees.

faq.question: Why do my miters have gaps?
faq.answer: Gaps usually come from: (1) the saw blade is not exactly at the set angle — check with a precision square, (2) boards are not perfectly flat or straight, (3) blade deflection on thick cuts. Use a sharp blade, make test cuts in scrap, and fine-tune the angle.

faq.question: How much glue should I use on a joint?
faq.answer: Apply enough for thin, even squeeze-out along the entire joint. For edge gluing, 4-6 mil thickness is ideal (about 200-250 sq ft per gallon). Too little starves the joint; too much creates a mess and does not add strength. Apply to both surfaces for end grain.

faq.question: Does wood glue expire?
faq.answer: PVA wood glue (Titebond) has a shelf life of 1-2 years unopened, 6-12 months after opening. Frozen or stringy glue should be discarded. Store between 50-80°F. Titebond III has better cold-weather tolerance than Titebond I or II.

faq.question: What angle should dovetails be?
faq.answer: Use 1:6 (about 9.5 degrees) for softwood and 1:8 (about 7 degrees) for hardwood. The steeper softwood angle provides more mechanical lock in weaker wood. Many hand-tool woodworkers use 1:7 as a general-purpose angle for all species.

faq.question: How many tails should a dovetail joint have?
faq.answer: The number of tails is aesthetic preference, but common practice is one tail per inch of board width minus one. A 6-inch board often has 3-4 tails. Wider tails look bolder and traditional; narrower tails look refined. Function is the same either way.

faq.question: How do I minimize plywood waste?
faq.answer: Try rotating parts 90 degrees to test both orientations. Group similar-sized parts together. Cut largest pieces first, then fit smaller pieces from remnants. Account for 1/8-inch blade kerf between cuts. Software optimizers can reduce waste from 30% to under 15%.

faq.question: What is a standard plywood sheet size?
faq.answer: Standard sheets are 4x8 feet (48x96 inches). Half sheets (4x4) are available at home centers. Specialty sizes include 5x5 Baltic birch. Actual dimensions may be 1/32 to 1/16 inch under nominal due to sanding and trimming.

faq.question: What is freight class and why does it matter?
faq.answer: Freight class (50-500) is an NMFC classification that determines LTL shipping rates. Class 50 (densest, easiest to handle) costs the least. Class 500 (lightest, bulkiest) costs the most. Moving down one class can save 10-15% on LTL rates.

faq.question: How is freight class determined?
faq.answer: Class is primarily based on density (lbs per cubic foot) but also considers handling difficulty, liability/value, and stowability. Some commodities have fixed NMFC classes regardless of density. Always verify with the NMFC tariff for your specific product code.

faq.question: What is dimensional weight?
faq.answer: Dimensional (DIM) weight prices bulky, lightweight packages by size rather than actual weight. Carriers divide cubic inches by a DIM divisor (139 for UPS/FedEx, 166 for USPS). You pay whichever is higher: actual or DIM weight.

faq.question: How can I reduce dimensional weight charges?
faq.answer: Use the smallest box possible. Eliminate excess void fill. Consider poly mailers for soft goods. Break large orders into multiple smaller packages. Negotiate a higher DIM divisor with your carrier for volume discounts.

faq.question: How is customs duty calculated?
faq.answer: U.S. customs duty is assessed on the CIF value (cost + insurance + freight). The duty rate depends on the HTS (Harmonized Tariff Schedule) code of the product. Rates range from 0% to 25%+ depending on product type and country of origin.

faq.question: Is there a duty-free threshold for imports?
faq.answer: The U.S. de minimis threshold is $800 per person per day for personal imports. Commercial shipments under $800 are generally duty-free. However, certain goods (alcohol, tobacco, specific regulated items) have no de minimis exemption.

faq.question: What is the maximum pallet weight for LTL shipping?
faq.answer: Most LTL carriers accept pallets up to 2,500 lbs, though some allow 3,000 lbs. The standard GMA pallet itself weighs 30-48 lbs. Keep in mind that forklift ratings and dock equipment may further limit weight in your facility.

faq.question: Should I interlock pallet layers?
faq.answer: Yes. Interlocking (rotating every other layer 90 degrees) significantly improves pallet stability during transit. Use a column-stacked pattern on the bottom layers for weight support, then interlock upper layers. Always stretch wrap the finished pallet.

faq.question: Is shipping insurance worth it?
faq.answer: If your loss/damage rate is above 1-2%, insurance pays for itself. High-value items ($200+) and fragile goods should always be insured. For low-value items under $50, self-insuring (absorbing occasional losses) is usually cheaper than per-package insurance.

faq.question: What does carrier declared value cover?
faq.answer: UPS and FedEx include $100 of declared value coverage automatically. USPS Priority includes $100 for domestic. Coverage pays actual value or declared value, whichever is less. Excluded items include jewelry, electronics, and artwork without additional coverage.

faq.question: What is a typical last mile delivery cost?
faq.answer: Last mile delivery accounts for 53% of total shipping costs. Average last mile cost in the U.S. is $8-12 per package for standard ground delivery. Same-day delivery can run $15-25. Dense urban areas cost less per stop than rural routes.

faq.question: How can I reduce last mile costs?
faq.answer: Strategies include: regional carrier partnerships (10-20% savings), ship-from-store, BOPIS (buy online, pick up in store), consolidation with other shippers, right-sizing packaging, and negotiating volume rates. Moving from national to regional carriers saves 15-25% on average.

faq.question: How much water do I need per day in an emergency?
faq.answer: FEMA recommends 1 gallon per person per day minimum — half for drinking, half for hygiene. In hot climates or with physical activity, plan 1.5-2 gallons. Nursing mothers and sick individuals need more. Store at least a 3-day supply, ideally 14 days.

faq.question: What is the best emergency water purification method?
faq.answer: Boiling (1 minute at rolling boil) is the most reliable method. Purification tablets (chlorine dioxide) are lightest for portable use. Pump filters remove bacteria and protozoa but not all viruses. Household bleach (8 drops per gallon, unscented) works in a pinch.

faq.question: What foods are best for long-term storage?
faq.answer: Top staples for long-term storage: white rice (30+ years), dried beans (25+ years), wheat berries (30+ years), rolled oats (25+ years), powdered milk (20+ years), honey (indefinite), salt (indefinite), and freeze-dried meals (25+ years). All require cool, dry, dark storage.

faq.question: How many calories should I store per day?
faq.answer: Store at least 2,000 calories per adult per day. Active adults need 2,500-3,000. Children need 1,200-1,800 depending on age. In cold weather, increase by 25-50% as your body burns more calories to stay warm. Always store more than minimum.

faq.question: How heavy should a bug-out bag be?
faq.answer: The general rule is 15-20% of your body weight for a 72-hour bag. A 180 lb person should target 27-36 lbs. Military packs often exceed 50 lbs, but soldiers train extensively. For civilians, lighter is better — you must be able to move quickly.

faq.question: What are the essential bug-out bag categories?
faq.answer: The core categories are: water (purification + container), shelter (tarp/bivy, sleeping system), fire (lighter, ferro rod, tinder), food (72 hrs of calorie-dense foods), first aid, navigation (map, compass), and communication (radio, whistle). Customize beyond these basics.

faq.question: How much water does a solar still produce?
faq.answer: A well-built 3-foot diameter solar still produces 8-32 oz per day depending on soil moisture, sun, and temperature. In ideal conditions with added vegetation or contaminated water, output improves. You typically need 3-5 stills to supplement one person's needs.

faq.question: How do I build a solar still?
faq.answer: Dig a 3-foot wide, 2-foot deep hole. Place a container in the center. Cover with clear plastic sheeting, seal edges with soil. Place a small rock in the center of the plastic above the container. Moisture evaporates, condenses on plastic, and drips into the container.

faq.question: What wood has the most BTU per cord?
faq.answer: Osage orange tops the list at ~32.9M BTU/cord. Hickory (30.6M), black locust (29.3M), and white oak (29.1M) are top heating woods. Softwoods like pine (17.1M) and cedar (13.0M) burn fast and are better for kindling than sustained heat.

faq.question: How many cords of wood do I need for winter?
faq.answer: A typical well-insulated 1,500 sq ft home in a cold climate (6,000+ heating degree days) uses 3-5 cords per heating season with a wood stove as primary heat. Mild climates may use 1-2 cords. An efficient EPA stove uses 30-40% less wood than an open fireplace.

faq.question: How far can a 5-watt radio reach?
faq.answer: A 5-watt UHF/VHF handheld typically reaches 3-5 miles in suburban terrain, 1-2 miles in urban areas, and 10-15 miles with line of sight from elevation. Advertised ranges of 25-35 miles are theoretical maximums achievable only under ideal conditions.

faq.question: What is the best emergency radio frequency?
faq.answer: For most emergencies: GMRS (requires license) offers 5W power and repeater access. FRS is license-free but limited to 0.5W. Ham radio (VHF/UHF with Technician license) gives the most flexibility. Channel 9 on CB (27 MHz) is the traditional emergency channel.

faq.question: How much does a bullet drop at 500 yards?
faq.answer: Drop varies enormously by cartridge. A .308 Win (175gr, 2600fps) drops about 50 inches at 500 yards zeroed at 100. A 6.5 Creedmoor (140gr, 2700fps) drops about 38 inches. A .223 (77gr, 2750fps) drops about 48 inches. Higher BC and velocity mean less drop.

faq.question: What is ballistic coefficient?
faq.answer: BC measures a bullet's ability to resist air drag. Higher BC means less drop and wind drift. Match-grade .30 cal bullets have BCs of 0.45-0.60 (G1). The G1 model assumes a flat-base bullet shape; G7 is more accurate for modern boat-tail bullets.

faq.question: How much does reloading save?
faq.answer: Savings depend on caliber. Common rifle (.308, .223) saves 30-50% vs. factory. Match-grade rifle saves 50-70%. Pistol (9mm) savings are smaller (20-30%) due to cheap factory ammo. Exotic calibers (.300 PRC, .338 Lapua) can save 60-75% per round.

faq.question: Is reloading worth it with equipment costs?
faq.answer: A quality single-stage press setup costs $400-800. Progressive press $600-1500. At 200 rounds/month saving $0.40/round, a $600 setup pays for itself in 7-8 months. High-volume pistol shooters recoup costs even faster with progressive presses.

faq.question: What happens if twist rate is too slow?
faq.answer: An understabilized bullet tumbles in flight, producing oblong or keyhole-shaped holes in targets. Accuracy degrades severely. Long, heavy-for-caliber bullets need faster twists. A 1:12 twist .223 won't stabilize 77gr bullets that need 1:8 or faster.

faq.question: Can twist be too fast?
faq.answer: Excessive spin can cause thin-jacketed bullets to come apart from centrifugal force, called 'spin disintegration.' Very fast twist with light varmint bullets at high velocity can cause this. Generally, over-stabilization is less problematic than under-stabilization.

faq.question: How much energy is needed for ethical hunting?
faq.answer: General minimums: deer-sized game needs 1,000+ ft-lbs at impact. Elk/moose need 1,500+ ft-lbs. Most states have no specific energy requirements but do regulate caliber minimums. Shot placement matters far more than raw energy for clean kills.

faq.question: What is the most powerful common rifle cartridge?
faq.answer: Among common sporting cartridges: .338 Lapua produces ~4,800 ft-lbs, .300 Win Mag ~3,500 ft-lbs, .30-06 ~2,900 ft-lbs, .308 Win ~2,600 ft-lbs, .223 Rem ~1,300 ft-lbs. Dangerous game cartridges like .458 Win Mag reach 5,000+ ft-lbs.

faq.question: What is MOA?
faq.answer: MOA (Minute of Angle) is an angular measurement equal to 1/60 of a degree. At 100 yards, 1 MOA equals approximately 1.047 inches. It scales linearly: at 200 yards 1 MOA = 2.094 inches, at 500 yards 1 MOA = 5.235 inches.

faq.question: Should I use MOA or Mil scopes?
faq.answer: MOA is more intuitive for shooters who think in inches (1 MOA ~1 inch at 100 yards). Mils pair naturally with metric measurements. Mils require fewer clicks for large adjustments. Military and competition shooters often prefer Mils. Either works — just be consistent.

faq.question: Which choke should I use for trap vs. skeet?
faq.answer: Skeet: Skeet or Improved Cylinder for close (20-25 yard) targets. Trap singles: Modified or Improved Modified for 30-35 yard targets. Trap handicap (longer range): Full or Improved Modified. Sporting clays: start with Modified and adjust based on station distance.

faq.question: What does choke constriction mean?
faq.answer: Choke constriction is the reduction in bore diameter at the muzzle. A 12-gauge bore is 0.729 inches. Full choke constricts to ~0.694 (0.035 reduction). Tighter constriction holds the shot column together longer, creating denser patterns at range but smaller effective patterns up close.

faq.question: How do I calculate resin for irregular shapes?
faq.answer: For irregular molds, fill the mold with water, then pour the water into a measuring cup. The volume in fluid ounces is the amount of mixed resin you need plus 10% for waste. For round molds, use pi x r^2 x depth to get cubic inches.

faq.question: Why is my resin not curing properly?
faq.answer: Most curing issues come from incorrect measuring. Always measure by volume (not weight) for 1:1 ratios. Mix thoroughly for 3-5 minutes, scraping sides and bottom. Temperature below 70F slows curing. Humidity above 80% can cause surface tackiness.

faq.question: How do I estimate yardage for a pattern?
faq.answer: Most patterns list total yardage needed. If not: knit a gauge swatch, measure the yarn used per square inch, then multiply by project area. Always buy 10% extra — dye lots vary between purchases, so buy all yarn at once.

faq.question: Does yarn weight affect yardage needed?
faq.answer: Yes. Bulky yarn covers more area per stitch but uses more yardage per square inch of fabric. A worsted-weight blanket might need 2,500 yards, while the same blanket in bulky needs about 3,000 yards. Lace weight uses less total yardage but takes much longer.

faq.question: How much fragrance oil should I add to candle wax?
faq.answer: Most waxes can hold 6-12% fragrance load. Soy wax typically maxes at 10-12%. Paraffin holds 6-10%. Start at 6% and increase to your preference. Too much fragrance causes sweating, poor burn, and wick clogging. Add fragrance at the correct temperature (usually 185F).

faq.question: Why do my candles have sinkholes?
faq.answer: Sinkholes form when the outer wax cools and solidifies before the inner wax, creating air pockets. Fix by pouring at a lower temperature (120-140F for soy), poking relief holes after initial set, and doing a second pour to fill gaps. Heating the container slightly before pouring helps.

faq.question: How much usable leather is in a full hide?
faq.answer: A full cowhide averages 45-55 sq ft, but 20-30% is typically unusable due to brand marks, scars, belly stretch, and irregular edges. Expect 33-42 sq ft of usable leather from a full hide. Premium hides have less waste.

faq.question: What leather thickness do I need?
faq.answer: Belts and holsters: 8-10 oz (3.2-4mm). Bags and cases: 4-6 oz (1.6-2.4mm). Wallets and linings: 2-3 oz (0.8-1.2mm). Garments: 1.5-3 oz (0.6-1.2mm). Leather is measured in ounces where 1 oz = 1/64 inch (0.4mm).

faq.question: How many strands should I use?
faq.answer: For 14-count Aida: use 2 strands for cross stitch, 1 strand for backstitch. For 18-count: 1-2 strands. For 11-count: 3 strands. The goal is full coverage without puffiness. More strands = faster coverage but less detail.

faq.question: How much thread is in a skein of DMC floss?
faq.answer: A standard DMC embroidery floss skein contains 8.7 yards (8 meters) of 6-strand thread. When separated into 2-strand lengths, you get about 26 yards of stitching thread per skein. One skein covers roughly 50-100 stitches on 14-count Aida.

faq.question: How thick should glaze be applied?
faq.answer: Standard application is about the thickness of a credit card (1-2mm). Three thin coats are better than one thick coat. Dip for 3 seconds or brush 3 coats with drying between each. Too thick causes crawling and running; too thin gives dry, rough surfaces.

faq.question: What is the correct glaze specific gravity?
faq.answer: Most glazes work best at 1.45-1.55 specific gravity (measured with a hydrometer). Dipping glazes should be thinner (1.40-1.50) and brushing glazes thicker (1.55-1.65). Adjust by adding water to thin or letting glaze settle and removing water to thicken.

faq.question: How much does cotton shrink?
faq.answer: Unwashed cotton typically shrinks 3-5% in both directions on the first wash. Some cotton fabrics can shrink up to 7%. Pre-washing before cutting is strongly recommended. Use the same wash/dry settings you plan to use for the finished garment.

faq.question: Should I pre-wash all fabric?
faq.answer: Pre-wash natural fibers (cotton, linen, wool, rayon) before cutting. Polyester and nylon rarely need pre-washing. For quilting, some quilters skip pre-washing to maintain fabric crispness and accept slight puckering after first wash for a vintage look.

faq.question: How wide should quilt binding strips be?
faq.answer: The standard is 2.5 inches for double-fold binding, which creates a finished binding of about 3/8 inch. For thicker quilts (batting + flannel), use 2.75 inch strips. Single-fold binding uses 1.75-2 inch strips. Cut strips on the straight grain unless binding curves.

faq.question: How much extra binding should I make?
faq.answer: Add 10-12 inches to the quilt perimeter for joining strips and turning corners. This accounts for the diagonal seams where strips are joined and a few inches of overlap at the starting/ending point. For bias binding, add an extra 15%.

faq.question: How much shorter should elastic be than my waist?
faq.answer: Cut waistband elastic to 85% of your waist measurement plus overlap (usually 1 inch). For a 30-inch waist: 30 x 0.85 + 1 = 26.5 inches. Some prefer 80% for snug fit. Test comfort by wrapping the elastic around your waist before cutting.

faq.question: What type of elastic is best for waistbands?
faq.answer: Knit elastic (braided or woven) is best for waistbands because it does not narrow when stretched. Braided elastic narrows and is better for casings. Fold-over elastic works for activewear. Use 1-1.5 inch width for adult waistbands.

faq.question: What is the standard seam allowance?
faq.answer: Commercial patterns (Simplicity, McCall's, Butterick) use 5/8 inch (1.5 cm). Quilting patterns use 1/4 inch. Independent/indie patterns vary — always check the instructions. European patterns often use 1 cm. Knit garments may use 3/8 inch.

faq.question: Why is 5/8 inch the standard?
faq.answer: 5/8 inch provides enough fabric to finish seams (serge, zigzag, or French seam) while not wasting material. It also allows room for fitting adjustments. Quilting uses 1/4 inch because quilts have many seams and bulk matters.

faq.question: Should I buy a longer zipper and shorten it?
faq.answer: Yes, for regular and invisible zippers. Buy 1-2 inches longer than needed and sew a new bottom stop with zigzag stitches. Separating zippers (jackets) cannot be shortened and must be the exact length. Continuous zippers are cut to any length.

faq.question: What zipper type for what project?
faq.answer: Invisible: dresses, skirts, formal wear. Regular all-purpose: casual clothing, home dec. Separating: jackets, hoodies. Continuous: bags, cushions, bedding. Metal: jeans, heavy garments. Molded plastic: sportswear, bags.

faq.question: When should I use fusible vs. sew-in interfacing?
faq.answer: Fusible interfacing is quicker and works for most fabrics. Use sew-in for: fabrics that cannot tolerate heat or steam (velvet, leather, sequins), very sheer fabrics where glue dots would show, and when you need softer drape in tailoring.

faq.question: How do I choose interfacing weight?
faq.answer: Match interfacing weight to your fabric or go one weight lighter. The interfacing should support without overpowering. Test on a scrap first. Light: blouses, facing. Medium: cotton dresses, collars. Heavy: waistbands, hats, bags. The finished piece should still drape naturally.

faq.question: Who actually pays tariffs?
faq.answer: The importing company pays tariffs to customs at the border. However, studies consistently show that most tariff costs (60-100%) are passed to consumers through higher prices. A 25% tariff on a $10 FOB item can increase retail price by $5-8 due to markup amplification.

faq.question: Why is the price increase larger than the tariff rate?
faq.answer: Tariffs are applied at the landed cost level, but each middleman (importer, distributor, retailer) applies their percentage markup to the already-tariffed cost. A 25% tariff becomes a 30-40% consumer price increase after each party takes their margin on the higher base cost.

calcFn: var margin = Math.max(0, ((normalValue - importPrice) / importPrice) * 100); var adPerUnit = importPrice * (margin / 100); var regDutyPerUnit = importPrice * (regularDuty / 100); var totalPerUnit = adPerUnit + regDutyPerUnit; var totalDuty = totalPerUnit * quantity; var effectiveRate = ((adPerUnit + regDutyPerUnit) / importPrice) * 100; document.getElementById('dumpingMargin').textContent = fmt(margin, 1) + '%'; document.getElementById('adDutyPerUnit').textContent = dollar(adPerUnit); document.getElementById('totalDuty').textContent = dollar(totalDuty); document.getElementById('effectiveRate').textContent = fmt(effectiveRate, 1) + '% combined';

AD Duty = Import Price x Dumping Margin %

faq.question: What is an anti-dumping duty?
faq.answer: An anti-dumping (AD) duty is a tariff imposed when a foreign company sells goods in the U.S. below their normal value (home market price). The duty equals the dumping margin. AD duties can range from 5% to over 400% and are in addition to regular tariffs.

faq.question: How long do anti-dumping duties last?
faq.answer: AD duties remain in effect indefinitely but are reviewed annually (administrative review) and every 5 years (sunset review). If dumping has stopped and the domestic industry is no longer injured, duties can be revoked. Most AD orders last 10-20+ years in practice.

tagline: Calculate potential savings from using a Foreign Trade Zone
keywords: free trade zone calculator, FTZ savings, foreign trade zone, duty deferral
calcFn: var annualDuty = annualImports * (dutyRate / 100); var reexportSave = annualDuty * (reexportPct / 100); var deferralDays = avgInventoryDays; var deferralSave = annualDuty * (interestRate / 100) * (deferralDays / 365); var weeklyEntrySave = 52 * 50; var total = reexportSave + deferralSave + weeklyEntrySave; document.getElementById('reexportSavings').textContent = dollar(reexportSave); document.getElementById('deferralSavings').textContent = dollar(deferralSave); document.getElementById('weeklyEntry').textContent = dollar(weeklyEntrySave) + ' (vs daily entries)'; document.getElementById('totalSavings').textContent = dollar(total);
formulas: Re-export Savings = Annual Duty x Re-export %
Deferral Savings = Annual Duty x Interest Rate x (Days / 365)
Weekly entry reduces customs broker fees by ~$50/week

faq.question: What is a Foreign Trade Zone?
faq.answer: An FTZ is a designated area within the U.S. where goods can be imported, stored, handled, manufactured, or reconfigured without paying customs duties until goods enter U.S. commerce. Goods re-exported from an FTZ avoid duties entirely. There are about 190 active FTZs in the U.S.

faq.question: When is an FTZ worth it?
faq.answer: FTZs are most valuable when: you re-export 15%+ of imports (full duty elimination), import high-duty goods with long inventory cycles (deferral savings), or use inverted tariff benefits (finished product duty lower than component duties). Annual savings of $50K+ typically justify FTZ activation costs.

tagline: Calculate duties under tariff rate quota systems
keywords: tariff rate quota, TRQ calculator, in-quota tariff, over-quota tariff
calcFn: var inQ = Math.min(importQuantity, quotaLimit); var overQ = Math.max(0, importQuantity - quotaLimit); var inDuty = inQ * unitPrice * (inQuotaRate / 100); var overDuty = overQ * unitPrice * (overQuotaRate / 100); var totalDuty = inDuty + overDuty; var totalValue = importQuantity * unitPrice; var effRate = (totalDuty / totalValue) * 100; document.getElementById('inQuotaQty').textContent = fmt(inQ, 0) + ' kg'; document.getElementById('overQuotaQty').textContent = fmt(overQ, 0) + ' kg'; document.getElementById('inQuotaDuty').textContent = dollar(inDuty); document.getElementById('overQuotaDuty').textContent = dollar(overDuty); document.getElementById('totalDuty').textContent = dollar(totalDuty); document.getElementById('effectiveRate').textContent = fmt(effRate, 1) + '%';
formulas: In-Quota Duty = min(Qty, Quota) x Price x In-Quota Rate
Over-Quota Duty = max(0, Qty - Quota) x Price x Over-Quota Rate

faq.question: What is a tariff rate quota?
faq.answer: A TRQ allows a limited quantity of imports at a low tariff rate (in-quota), with quantities above the limit subject to a much higher rate (over-quota). For example, U.S. sugar imports face 1.4 cents/kg in-quota vs. 33.87 cents/kg over-quota. TRQs are common for agricultural products.

faq.question: How are TRQ allocations distributed?
faq.answer: TRQs are typically allocated by country (country-specific quotas) or on a first-come-first-served basis. Some quotas are allocated by historical market share. Importers must present quota certificates at time of entry. Once the quota fills, subsequent imports pay the over-quota rate.

tagline: Determine if your product qualifies for preferential tariff treatment
keywords: rules of origin calculator, origin determination, substantial transformation, preferential tariff
calcFn: var req = parseFloat(requiredPct); var origContent = domesticContent + directLabor + overhead; var rvcPct = (origContent / totalValue) * 100; var nonOrig = totalValue - origContent; var qualifies = rvcPct >= req ? 'QUALIFIES — meets ' + req + '% threshold' : 'DOES NOT QUALIFY — need ' + fmt(req - rvcPct, 1) + '% more'; var dutySave = rvcPct >= req ? totalValue * 0.05 : 0; document.getElementById('rvc').textContent = fmt(rvcPct, 1) + '%'; document.getElementById('nonOriginating').textContent = dollar(nonOrig) + ' (' + fmt((nonOrig/totalValue)*100, 1) + '%)'; document.getElementById('qualifies').textContent = qualifies; document.getElementById('dutySavings').textContent = dollar(dutySave) + ' (at est. 5% duty)';
formulas: RVC = (Originating Materials + Labor + Overhead) / Total Value x 100
Product qualifies if RVC >= Required Percentage

faq.question: What are rules of origin?
faq.answer: Rules of origin determine the 'nationality' of a product for tariff purposes. To qualify for preferential rates under a free trade agreement, products must meet minimum local/regional content requirements. Methods include Regional Value Content (RVC), tariff shift, and net cost.

faq.question: What is the USMCA origin requirement?
faq.answer: Under USMCA (replacing NAFTA), most goods need 75% regional value content. Automobiles have specific rules: 75% RVC for vehicles, 70% for core parts (engines, transmissions), and 65% for other parts. Steel and aluminum must be melted and poured in North America.

tagline: Calculate the total duty including Section 301 tariffs on Chinese goods
keywords: Section 301 tariff, China tariff calculator, trade war tariff, 301 duty
calcFn: var s301 = parseFloat(section301Rate); var baseDuty = productValue * (baseDutyRate / 100); var s301Duty = productValue * (s301 / 100); var totalDuty = baseDuty + s301Duty; var effRate = ((baseDutyRate + s301) ); var landedPerUnit = (productValue + totalDuty) / quantity; document.getElementById('baseDuty').textContent = dollar(baseDuty); document.getElementById('s301Duty').textContent = dollar(s301Duty); document.getElementById('totalDuty').textContent = dollar(totalDuty); document.getElementById('effectiveRate').textContent = fmt(effRate, 1) + '%'; document.getElementById('landedCost').textContent = dollar(landedPerUnit) + '/unit';
formulas: Total Duty = (Base Rate + Section 301 Rate) x CIF Value
Section 301 stacks on top of regular duties

faq.question: What is Section 301?
faq.answer: Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974 authorizes the President to impose tariffs on countries engaged in unfair trade practices. The current Section 301 tariffs target Chinese goods in response to IP theft and forced technology transfer. Rates range from 7.5% to 100% depending on product category.

faq.question: Can I get an exclusion from Section 301 tariffs?
faq.answer: The USTR periodically opens exclusion request processes for specific products. Exclusions are granted when the product is only available from China, tariffs cause severe economic harm, or the product is not strategically important. Approved exclusions are retroactive and temporary (usually 1 year, renewable).
